summary: Stancil argues that Google has amassed an impressive but incoherent set of data products, like a kid with powerful Pokemon cards but no cohesive deck. The opportunity is to define a clear buyer (the "purple people" in data orgs) and sell an integrated, easy-to-buy stack rather than overpowered but disconnected tools, a miss driven by tech-first culture and compute-driven sales.
money_quotes: In the data industry, Google is this kid.
As far as data technologies go, it’s an embarrassment of riches. But like that of the kid with the Pokémon deck of death, Google’s strategy is much harder to discern.
Modularity, in other words, often isn’t as valuable as convenience.
In the multifaceted, multimodal, multiplayer world of enterprise data, it’s the glue that matters the most.
Google has the technology to build it, and the capital to buy it. What’s less clear is if they have the vision to imagine it.
key_insight: Google’s data stack has the pieces to dominate, but without a coherent buyer focus and integrated experience, its overpowered tools fail to translate into market leadership.
